critic_raw:
YNet's trajectory forecasting methodology presents several interconnected evaluation and distributional concerns.

The model conditions on segmentation maps and past trajectory heatmaps. The segmentation maps encode semantic environment structure (walkable areas, obstacles), which requires either ground-truth segmentation at test time or a pre-trained segmentation model. The paper does not characterize segmentation quality degradation: if the segmentation model makes errors on novel environments, YNet inherits those errors without reporting how sensitive trajectory predictions are to segmentation quality. This is a silent failure mode in deployment — the trajectory loss degrades without any error signal from the trajectory component.

The two-stage prediction (goal prediction followed by path prediction conditioned on waypoints) introduces an error propagation structure that aggregate FDE/ADE metrics on SDD and ETH/UCY cannot capture. If the goal prediction is wrong, all path predictions conditioned on that goal will be systematically biased toward the wrong region of space. The reported metrics average over all samples including cases where goal prediction was correct; a model that performs well on cases with easy goal prediction can achieve good aggregate metrics while failing completely on ambiguous goal scenarios. Evidence needed: report FDE/ADE conditioned on goal prediction accuracy.

The Stanford Drone Dataset (SDD) and ETH/UCY datasets have known distribution properties: SDD is captured from aerial perspective on a campus, ETH/UCY from pedestrian-level cameras. These viewpoint differences mean the implicit spatial scale calibration differs between datasets. The model uses a shared architecture across both — but the spatial relationship between the segmentation map and the trajectory heatmap has different resolution characteristics at different viewpoints. The paper does not assess how the spatial resolution mismatch between datasets affects the encoding quality.

The claim that multi-modal trajectory prediction addresses 'epistemic factors such as pedestrian intent' conflates modeling multi-modal distributions with capturing intent. The model produces multiple trajectory samples from a stochastic component, but sampling from a learned mixture is not equivalent to modeling intent: the learned distribution reflects the training data's empirical trajectory diversity, not individual agent intentions. This matters for evaluation: FDE at k=20 (best-of-k) rewards trajectory diversity regardless of whether the diverse predictions reflect genuine intent modeling.
all_issues_raised: YNet conditions on segmentation maps whose quality is never characterized; errors in the upstream segmentation model silently degrade trajectory predictions without any error signal from the trajectory component, making the method brittle to novel environments with weaker segmentation quality.

The two-stage prediction pipeline (goal then path) has error propagation structure invisible to aggregate ADE/FDE: wrong goal predictions cause systematic path biases, but averaging over all samples including easy cases hides this failure mode.

SDD (aerial viewpoint) and ETH/UCY (pedestrian-level viewpoint) have different spatial scale calibrations; the shared architecture must handle segmentation-to-trajectory resolution relationships that differ by dataset, and this source of variance is not analyzed.

FDE at k=20 (best-of-k metric) rewards any trajectory distribution with high diversity regardless of causal mechanism; claiming this demonstrates 'epistemic intent modeling' conflates empirical trajectory diversity with genuinely captured agent intent.
all_issues_adjudicated: YNet conditions on segmentation maps whose quality is never characterized; errors in the upstream segmentation model silently degrade trajectory predictions without any error signal from the trajectory component, making the method brittle to novel environments with weaker segmentation quality.

The two-stage prediction pipeline (goal then path) has error propagation structure invisible to aggregate ADE/FDE: wrong goal predictions cause systematic path biases, but averaging over all samples including easy cases hides this failure mode.

SDD (aerial viewpoint) and ETH/UCY (pedestrian-level viewpoint) have different spatial scale calibrations; the shared architecture must handle segmentation-to-trajectory resolution relationships that differ by dataset, and this source of variance is not analyzed.

FDE at k=20 (best-of-k metric) rewards any trajectory distribution with high diversity regardless of causal mechanism; claiming this demonstrates 'epistemic intent modeling' conflates empirical trajectory diversity with genuinely captured agent intent.
